1.pericope_label: The Seventh Seal: Silence, Incense, and Fire
1.exposition: The Lamb breaks the last seal, and heaven — which has not ceased its worship since the throne-room opened — falls utterly still. There was silence in heaven for about half an hour: the hush is the hinge of the whole book, the pause between the completed seals and the trumpets they contain. Into that silence step seven angels with seven trumpets (v.2), and then another angel at the golden altar, offering much incense with the prayers of all the saints (vv.3–4). The prayers rise; and then the same censer that carried them up is filled with fire from the altar and hurled to the earth, and heaven answers with thunder, voices, lightning, and earthquake (v.5). The order is deliberate and startling: the trumpet-judgments about to fall are the answer to the church's prayers — the fire of the altar of intercession loosed upon the world.
1.original_language: The silence is named with a word found nowhere else in the New Testament — hēmiōrion, “half an hour” (v.1). grammatical-historical Robertson savors the rare compound and hears in egeneto sigē, “there came a silence,” a dramatic cessation — no elder, no chorus of praise, no thunder from the throne. The angel's libanōtos (v.3) is properly the vessel of frankincense, here a golden censer; the thymiama (“incense”) is added to the prayers upon the thysiastērion, the golden altar of incense that stood before the throne — distinct from the altar of burnt offering beneath which the martyrs' souls had cried.
1.historical_context: The whole scene is built from temple worship. Morning and evening a priest entered the holy place to burn incense on the golden altar while the assembled people prayed outside in silence — the very tableau of Luke 1:10. grammatical-historical Clarke presses the detail: on the Day of Atonement the high priest carried fire from the great altar in a golden censer, and the people's silent prayer is mirrored in this “silence in heaven.” The seven angels “who stand before God” reflect the heavenly court of Jewish tradition — the seven archangels who present the prayers of the saints, and the Gabriel who “stands in the presence of God” (Luke 1:19). The trumpet was Israel's instrument of alarm, muster, and war (Numbers 10:9), the horn that felled Jericho (Joshua 6:4). And the fire flung earthward recalls the burning coals scattered over the city from between the cherubim in Ezekiel 10:2 — the altar's own fire become judgment.
1.christ: The angel at the altar has divided readers for centuries. reformed Gill and puritan Matthew Henry read him as Christ himself, the great High Priest, whose “much incense” is the fulness of his own merit and mediation, making the saints' imperfect prayers acceptable. Whether or not the figure is Christ, the logic is unmistakably his: the prayers reach God not on their own worth but perfumed by another — precisely the ministry of the one who “always lives to make intercession” (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34), whose self-offering rose to God as a fragrant aroma (Ephesians 5:2). The saints do not approach the throne alone.

verses.note: When the Lamb opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven for about half an hour — and the ceaseless worship of the throne suddenly stops. The witnesses hear the hush in different keys. patristic Victorinus takes it for the beginning of everlasting rest, only “partial” because the silence is broken and the story resumes in the trumpets. puritan Matthew Henry offers a double reading: a silence of peace, when no cry of the oppressed church rises to God, or a silence of expectation — heaven and earth still, as in “Be silent, O all flesh, before the Lord” (Zechariah 2:13; Psalms 46:10). Here the great structural question of the book surfaces too: patristic Bede reads the seventh seal as the point where John “recapitulates from the beginning,” about to relate the same things in another manner, while grammatical-historical Barnes insists the contrary — the trumpets are nested within the seventh seal and follow the seals in sequence, like the drawing out of a telescope's last section.

John sees the seven angels who stand before God, and to them are given seven trumpets. reformed Gill distinguishes them from the seven spirits of God and from the ministers of the word: these are angelic spirits standing ready in God's immediate presence to do his will. grammatical-historical Barnes lingers on the definite article — “the seven angels” — and on their permanent station, like the Gabriel who “stands in the presence of God” (Luke 1:19). The trumpet is the instrument of summons and alarm; armed each with one, the angels are appointed to sound God's judgments forth in turn.

Another angel stands at the golden altar with a censer, and is given much incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints — and over his identity the witnesses honestly divide. reformed Gill and puritan Matthew Henry read him as no created angel but Christ himself, the great High Priest, the “much incense” being the fulness of his own merit by which imperfect prayers are made acceptable. grammatical-historical Ellicott resists the identification — “another angel” tells against it, and the figure is symbolic — yet grants the truth it carries: the incense is first given to the angel before he adds it, so the prayers are made sweet not by the saints' worth but by a fragrance supplied from God. Either way, no prayer thus recommended is ever denied audience.

The smoke of the incense, with the prayers of the saints, ascends before God out of the angel's hand. reformed Gill pictures the incense kindled on the coals until its cloud fills the whole temple; so the prayers, perfumed, rise acceptably into the very presence of God. This is the portrait of accepted prayer: the believer's petitions, offered through the great Intercessor, do not fall spent to the ground but reach the throne itself — and, as the next verse shows at once, they move heaven to act.

Then the startling turn: the angel fills the same censer with fire from the altar and casts it to the earth, and there follow thunders, voices, lightnings, and an earthquake. grammatical-historical Barnes marks it as a new symbol — the censer that bore the prayers up now bears fire down in judgment. puritan Matthew Henry draws the connection tight: this is God's answer to the prayers of the saints, the fire of the altar of intercession loosed as vengeance on a persecuting world, making good the martyrs' cry of 6:10. The coals scattered over the city from between the cherubim in Ezekiel 10:2 flare again here, and the thunder-and-earthquake formula announces that God has risen to judge.

witnesses.point: The half-hour silence is the beginning of everlasting rest — but only “partial,” since it is broken and the narrative resumes in the trumpets.
At the seventh seal John “recapitulates from the beginning,” about to relate the same things in another manner; the silence is the church's Sabbath rest after conflict.
The angel at the altar is Christ the great High Priest, offering the saints' prayers perfumed with the much incense of his own merit and mediation.
The prayers of the saints, accepted through Christ's incense, produce great changes on earth: the same censer that bore them up casts the altar-fire down in judgment.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The prayers of the saints offered with the incense are not a passive backdrop but the trigger of the trumpet judgments — the persecuted church's cry for God's kingdom, rising before the throne, sets heaven's answer in motion.

1.application: Heaven falls silent, and then heaven acts — because the prayers of the saints have reached the throne. The very fire that carried their cries upward is flung back as God's answer upon the earth. Your prayers, offered through the one whose incense makes them sweet, are neither lost nor idle; they are gathered before God and, in his time, move the very engines of history.

6.exposition: The seven angels lift their trumpets, and the new-Exodus plagues begin to fall. The first four strike the four quarters of creation in turn — land, sea, fresh water, and sky. Hail and fire mingled with blood burn a third of the earth (v.7); a blazing mountain turns a third of the sea to blood and wrecks its ships (vv.8–9); the star Wormwood poisons a third of the rivers (vv.10–11); and a third of sun, moon, and stars is struck dark (v.12). Again and again the refrain is “the third part” — the judgments are real but bounded, partial and not yet final, warnings meant to bring the earth to repentance. Then a lone eagle wheels across midheaven crying “Woe, woe, woe” (v.13), dividing the trumpets: the four that have sounded were grievous, but the three still to come are of another and more dreadful order.
6.original_language: The judgments fall by fractions — to triton, “the third,” tolls through the passage like a refrain, the measured portion that the seals (a quarter, 6:8) fall short of and the bowls will exceed. The third star is named Apsinthos, “Wormwood” (v.11), the bitter plant Scripture makes the very taste of covenant judgment (Jeremiah 9:15). And the herald of v.13 is, in the better manuscripts, not an angel but an aetos, an eagle — a bird of prey whose scream over a battlefield suits the threefold ouai, “woe,” far better than a messenger's greeting.
6.historical_context: The four trumpets are deliberately shaped as the plagues of Egypt fallen on a new Pharaoh. The hail mingled with fire replays the seventh plague (Exodus 9:24); the sea turned to blood, the first (Exodus 7:20); the darkened lights, the ninth (Exodus 10:22). As the plagues warned Egypt before the Exodus, so these warn the world — yet, unlike Egypt's, they strike only “a third,” held in check by God's own hand. The older historicist expositors pressed each into a chapter of Rome's fall: the first trumpet the barbarian invasions, the second a maritime power broken, the third a fallen ruler or teacher poisoning the sources of life. The name Wormwood itself names the effect — reformed Gill recalls Naomi, who called herself Mara, “bitter,” because the Almighty had dealt bitterly with her (Ruth 1:20).
6.christ: These are the Lamb's judgments, not the Father's wrath bypassing the Son: they pour from the very scroll that only the Lamb was found worthy to open. The new-Exodus pattern is itself christological — as the plagues on Egypt went before Israel's redemption and Pharaoh's overthrow, so these plagues go before the Lamb's final victory and the liberation of the people he has purchased. And the mercy hidden in the “third” is the Lamb's own: patristic Victorinus hears in the flying herald of v.13 the Spirit's witness that, even in the last times, whoever will turn may yet be saved.

verses.note: The altar-fire cast down, the seven angels who had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound. grammatical-historical Barnes pictures them arranging themselves in order, to sound not all at once but in succession, one after another. patristic Bede hears already the trumpets of the church, which — like the horns that compassed Jericho seven days — are the confident preaching by which the glory of the world is thrown down. The verse is the threshold: all heaven poised, waiting upon God's signal.

The first trumpet: hail and fire mingled with blood, and a third of the trees and all the green grass are burnt up. puritan Matthew Henry calls the events dismal — a terrible storm of fiery, bloody judgment, whether taken literally or figuratively. grammatical-historical Barnes reads it as fiery wrath poured upon the earth, and, with the historicist expositors, applies it to the desolating invasions that fell upon the Roman empire. Behind both stands the seventh Egyptian plague, hail and fire running along the ground (Exodus 9:24) — Egypt's judgment fallen now upon the wider world.

The second trumpet: a great mountain burning with fire is cast into the sea, and a third of it becomes blood. grammatical-historical Barnes, in the historicist reading, takes the blazing mass for some great destroying power hurled against a maritime part of the empire; the sea turned to blood recalls the first plague of the Nile (Exodus 7:20). The figure is of a vast, fiery ruin — a token of slaughter loosed upon the deep.

The mountain's fall has a twofold effect: a third of the living creatures in the sea die, and a third of the ships are destroyed. reformed Gill, reading historically, takes the sea-creatures for men — the inhabitants of the empire — and the ships for its commerce and wealth, so the verse figures both the death of multitudes and the ruin of trade. Yet, as at every trumpet, the destruction is held to “the third part,” bounded and not total.

The third trumpet: a great star, burning like a lamp, falls upon a third of the rivers and the fountains of waters. grammatical-historical Barnes notes that a star is a natural emblem of a prince or ruler, and reads its fall as the ruin of some great leader — a fall that corrupts the very springs from which the people drink. The judgment moves from sea to fresh water, striking the sources of life themselves.

The star's name is Wormwood, and a third of the waters turn bitter, so that many die of them. reformed Gill notes the star is named for the bitter afflictions it brings, as Naomi named herself Mara because the Almighty had dealt bitterly with her (Ruth 1:20). Whether the bitterness is read as calamity or as corrupt teaching poisoning the springs of souls, the sweet waters that should give life now deal death — the very taste of covenant judgment God once threatened to make his people drink (Jeremiah 9:15).

The fourth trumpet: a third of the sun, moon, and stars is smitten and darkened, so that a third of day and night gives no light. The plague of darkness over Egypt sounds again here (Exodus 10:22). reformed Gill records the reading that the smiting of the great lights figures the obscuring of rulers, teachers, and the very light of truth — some referring it to the darkening of the doctrine of Christ's person through heresy. Whatever the application, a measured darkness falls upon the lights meant to guide.

Between the fourth trumpet and the fifth, John sees a lone flier crying with a loud voice, “Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth”. reformed Gill notes that the better manuscripts read not “angel” but “eagle,” which suits the flight, and that the cry sets the last three trumpets apart as heavier “woe” trumpets. The threefold woe answers to the three yet to sound, each falling on “the inhabiters of the earth” — Revelation's own name for the unrepentant who have thrown in their lot with the world. patristic Victorinus, however, hears the flying herald as the Spirit's warning, given so that even now, in the last times, any who will may still turn and be saved.

witnesses.point: The first four trumpets form a distinct group of desolating judgments; the historicist reads them of the successive blows that brought down the Roman empire.
Wormwood is named for the bitterness it brings, as Naomi called herself Mara; the darkened lights may figure the obscuring of truth by heresy.
The first trumpet's storm of hail and fire mingled with blood is a dismal, desolating judgment, whether understood literally or figuratively.
The eagle flying in midheaven is the Spirit's witness that great wrath is imminent — a warning so that, even in the last times, any who will may be saved.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The first four trumpets deliberately reprise the Exodus plagues, now fallen on the idolatrous world rather than on Egypt; the recurring “third” marks them as partial, non-final judgments meant to warn and summon to repentance, not yet to destroy.

6.application: The judgments come by thirds — real, but restrained. Every partial blow is a summons: a world still able to repent is being warned before the worst can fall. The mercy is in the limit. Do not despise the trumpet that stops short of the end; it sounds precisely so that some, hearing it, may turn while there is still time.

_meta.title: Silence in Heaven, and the Trumpets of Warning
reflection: Before the trumpets sound, the prayers of the saints rise like incense and the altar-fire is flung to earth (vv.3–5). What changes when you believe your prayers are gathered before God and move his hand in history?

The first four trumpets strike only “a third” — bounded warnings, not the end (vv.7–12). Where have you treated a limited hardship as mere misfortune, when it may have been a summons to return to God?

The flying herald cries “Woe, woe, woe” over those who dwell on the earth, yet the same warning is spoken so that any may still be saved (v.13). How do God's warnings of judgment, rightly heard, function as an invitation of mercy?
